description: Main economic definitions and formulas that are not part of the units, physics, math or chemistry base data.

words.name: economics
words.description: social science that studies the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services

words.name: microeconomics
words.description: branch of economics that studies the behavior of individual households and firms in making decisions on the allocation of limited resources

words.name: macroeconomics
words.description: branch of economics that studies aggregated indicators

words.name: econometrics
words.description: academic discipline

words.name: supply
words.description: in economics, the amount of a good that sellers are willing to provide in the market

words.name: demand
words.description: in economics, the market force that causes buyers to be both willing and able to purchase a good or service, measured by the amount of that good or service that is currently salable at any given price point

words.name: price
words.description: quantity of payment or compensation given by one party to another in return for goods or services; the proposed value for such a quantity as proposed by the seller

words.name: market
words.description: mechanisms whereby supply and demand confront each other and deals are made, involving places, processes and institutions in which exchanges occur (for physical venues, use Q132510 or Q330284)

words.name: elasticity
words.description: responsiveness of one economic variable to a change in another (e.g. supply or demand as a function of changed price, cross-price, income, wealth, advertising, etc.), ranging from inelastic to elastic

words.name: utility
words.description: concept in economics & game theory: a relative measure of happiness or satisfaction

words.name: capital
words.description: assets used for the production of goods and services

words.name: entrepreneurship
words.description: creation or extraction of economic value

words.name: cost
words.description: value of money that has been used up to produce something

words.name: revenue
words.description: income that a business has from its normal business activities

words.name: profit
words.description: concept in economics

words.name: markup
words.description: ratio of the cost of a good or service and its selling price

words.name: monopoly
words.description: market structure with a single firm dominating the market

words.name: oligopoly
words.description: market form in which a market or industry is dominated by a small number of sellers

words.name: externality
words.description: impact on any party not involved in a given economic transaction or act

words.name: consumption
words.description: purchase and use of goods and services

words.name: saving
words.description: the portion of income that is not spent on current consumption

words.name: investment
words.description: commitment of resources to achieve later benefits

words.name: inflation
words.description: rise in price level in an economy over time

words.name: deflation
words.description: decrease in the general price level of goods and services

words.name: unemployment
words.description: state of being without work and actively seeking work

words.name: workforce
words.description: members of a particular organization or population who are able or eligible to work; labour pool in employment; it may also mean all those who are available for work

words.name: recession
words.description: business cycle contraction

words.name: tariff
words.description: tax on the import and export of goods

words.name: subsidy
words.description: financial support to an economic sector

words.name: tax
words.description: mandatory financial charge or other type of levy imposed on a taxpayer (an individual or legal entity) by a governmental organization to collectively fund government spending, public expenditures, or to regulate and reduce negative externalities

words.name: depreciation
words.description: allocating the cost of a (tangible) asset over a period of time

words.name: asset
words.description: economic resource, from which future economic benefits are expected

words.name: liability
words.description: a present obligation of an entity to transfer an economic benefit, shown on the balance sheet

words.name: equity
words.description: difference between the value of the assets/interest and the cost of the liabilities of something owned

words.name: debt
words.description: deferred payment, or series of payments, that is owed in the future

words.name: money
words.description: object or record accepted as payment

words.name: wage
words.description: the payment made by an employer to an employee in return for work performed

words.name: population
words.description: the number of people living in a given country or region

words.name: quantity
words.description: property that exists in a range of magnitudes or multitudes; property that can exist as a magnitude or multitude

words.name: example
words.description: a representative instance used to illustrate a rule or calculation

words.name: textbook
words.description: a book used as a standard work for the study of a subject

triples.name: social science
triples.description: academic disciplines concerned with society and the relationships between individuals in society. see Wikipedia: Social science (Q34749)

triples.name: behavioral economics
triples.description: discipline of economy studying the effects of psychological, cognitive, emotional, cultural and social factors on decisions. see Wikipedia: Behavioral economics (Q647525)

triples.name: international economics
triples.description: academic discipline. see Wikipedia: International economics (Q47417)

triples.name: public economics
triples.description: study of government economic and fiscal policy. see Wikipedia: Public economics (Q2248246)

triples.name: labour economics
triples.description: functioning and dynamics of the markets for labour. see Wikipedia: Labour economics (Q28161)

triples.name: financial economics
triples.description: branch of economics concerned with financial or monetary transactions. see Wikipedia: Financial economics (Q2294553)

triples.name: monetary economics
triples.description: branch of economics that studies different theories of money. see Wikipedia: Monetary economics (Q580018)

triples.name: development economics
triples.description: economics of developing economies. see Wikipedia: Development economics (Q1127188)

triples.name: economic equilibrium
triples.description: state where economic forces such as supply and demand are balanced and the values of economic variables will not change. see Wikipedia: Economic equilibrium (Q836133)
responsiveness of the quantity demanded of a good or service to a change in its price. see Wikipedia: Price elasticity of demand (Q3194154)
responsiveness of the quantity supplied of a good or service to a change in its price. see Wikipedia: Price elasticity of supply (Q2620132)
responsiveness of demand for goods with respect to income change. see Wikipedia: Income elasticity of demand (Q734113)
responsiveness in the quantity demand of one good when a change in price takes place in another good. see Wikipedia: Cross elasticity of demand (Q372919)

triples.name: marginal utility
triples.description: change in satisfaction resulting from an increase or decrease in consumption of one unit of a good or service. see Wikipedia: Marginal utility (Q842271)

triples.name: economic surplus
triples.description: monetary benefit that accrues to parties to an economic transaction. see Wikipedia: Economic surplus (Q268617)

triples.name: deadweight loss
triples.description: measure of lost economic efficiency. see Wikipedia: Deadweight loss (Q358063)

triples.name: opportunity cost
triples.description: when presented with multiple opportunities, the cost of making a certain choice in comparison to its alternatives. see Wikipedia: Opportunity cost (Q185715)

triples.name: comparative advantage
triples.description: in economics, the advantage one has over others in producing a particular good due to a lower relative marginal cost prior to trade. see Wikipedia: Comparative advantage (Q215551)

triples.name: absolute advantage
triples.description: economic concept. see Wikipedia: Absolute advantage (Q332593)

the cost advantages that enterprises obtain due to size, throughput, or scale of operation, with cost per unit of output generally decreasing with increasing scale as fixed costs are spread out over more units of output. see Wikipedia: Economies of scale (Q5970087)

triples.name: diminishing returns
triples.description: additional factor of production resulting in smaller increase in output. see Wikipedia: Diminishing returns (Q863946)
the resources used in the production of goods and services, such as land, labour and capital. see Wikipedia: Factors of production (Q210947)

triples.name: fixed cost
triples.description: business expenses that are not dependent on the level of goods or services produced by the business. see Wikipedia: Fixed cost (Q466764)

triples.name: variable cost
triples.description: costs that change in proportion to the good or service that a business produces. see Wikipedia: Variable cost (Q174915)

triples.name: total cost
triples.description: total economic cost of production. see Wikipedia: Total cost (Q1289588)

triples.name: marginal cost
triples.description: factor in economics. see Wikipedia: Marginal cost (Q382444)

triples.name: average cost
triples.description: factor in economics. see Wikipedia: Average cost (Q945621)

triples.name: marginal revenue
triples.description: term used in microeconomics. see Wikipedia: Marginal revenue (Q841032)
carrying value of goods sold during a particular period. see Wikipedia: Cost of goods sold (Q12154181)

triples.name: gross margin
triples.description: relating gross profits to net sales. see Wikipedia: Gross margin (Q11157999)

triples.name: profit margin
triples.description: ratio between turnover and profit. see Wikipedia: Profit margin (Q1187906)

triples.name: break-even point
triples.description: equality of costs and revenues. see Wikipedia: Break-even point (Q626990)

triples.name: contribution margin
triples.description: cost accounting term. see Wikipedia: Contribution margin (Q1545019)

triples.name: perfect competition
triples.description: market structure in which firms are price takers for a homogenous product. see Wikipedia: Perfect competition (Q183384)

triples.name: monopolistic competition
triples.description: imperfect competition of differentiated products that are not perfect substitutes. see Wikipedia: Monopolistic competition (Q379579)

triples.name: market failure
triples.description: situation in which the allocation of goods and services by a free market is not efficient and can be improved upon from the societal point of view, often leading to a net loss of economic value. see Wikipedia: Market failure (Q622657)

triples.name: public good
triples.description: good that is non-excludable and non-rival. see Wikipedia: Public good (economics) (Q272458)

triples.name: gross domestic product
triples.description: market value of goods and services produced within a country. see Wikipedia: Gross domestic product (Q12638)

triples.name: gross national product
triples.description: total domestic and foreign output claimed by residents of a country, consisting of gross domestic product, plus factor incomes earned by foreign residents, minus income earned in the domestic economy by nonresidents. see Wikipedia: Gross national product (Q6803767)

triples.name: real gross domestic product
triples.description: macroeconomic measure of the value of economic output adjusted for price changes. see Wikipedia: Real gross domestic product (Q7301159)

triples.name: GDP deflator
triples.description: measure of the level of prices of all new, domestically produced, final goods and services in an economy, with respect to a specific base year, but not based on a fixed basket of goods and services. see Wikipedia: GDP deflator (Q796024)

triples.name: government spending
triples.description: government consumption, investment, and transfer payments. see Wikipedia: Government spending (Q1929688)

triples.name: aggregate demand
triples.description: total amount of demand for goods and services in an economy. see Wikipedia: Aggregate demand (Q1801078)

triples.name: aggregate supply
triples.description: economic concept. see Wikipedia: Aggregate supply (Q2474789)

triples.name: consumer price index
triples.description: indicates tracking prices of consumer goods as an economic measure. see Wikipedia: Consumer price index (Q180687)

triples.name: economic growth
triples.description: measure of increase in market value of goods. see Wikipedia: Economic growth (Q189833)

triples.name: business cycle
triples.description: fluctuation in the degree of utilization of the production potential of an economy. see Wikipedia: Business cycle (Q192311)

triples.name: fiscal policy
triples.description: use of government revenue collection and spending to influence the economy. see Wikipedia: Fiscal policy (Q187021)

triples.name: monetary policy
triples.description: subclass of the economic policy. see Wikipedia: Monetary policy (Q178476)

triples.name: money supply
triples.description: component of macroeconomics. see Wikipedia: Money supply (Q842206)

triples.name: monetary base
triples.description: portion of the commercial banks' reserves that are maintained in accounts with their central bank plus the total currency circulating in the public. see Wikipedia: Monetary base (Q593105)
rate of money changing hands. see Wikipedia: Velocity of money (Q1323540)

triples.name: reserve requirement
triples.description: type of regulation on commercial banks. see Wikipedia: Reserve requirement (Q1058762)

triples.name: money multiplier
triples.description: ratio of money generated by banking system to central bank's monetary base. see Wikipedia: Money multiplier (Q1499566)
theory in monetary economics. see Wikipedia: Quantity theory of money (Q515911)

triples.name: interest rate
triples.description: percentage of a sum of money charged for its use. see Wikipedia: Interest rate (Q179179)

triples.name: nominal interest rate
triples.description: concept in finance and economics. see Wikipedia: Nominal interest rate (Q1205832)

triples.name: real interest rate
triples.description: the interest rate after taking inflation into account. see Wikipedia: Real interest rate (Q1140421)

triples.name: exchange rate
triples.description: rate at which one currency will be exchanged for another. see Wikipedia: Exchange rate (Q66100)

triples.name: purchasing power parity
triples.description: economic theory that states that the exchange rate between two countries is equal to the ratio of the currencies' respective purchasing power. see Wikipedia: Purchasing power parity (Q82135)
difference between the monetary value of exports and imports. see Wikipedia: Balance of trade (Q192796)

triples.name: current account
triples.description: concept in international economics. see Wikipedia: Current account (balance of payments) (Q746687)
metric in economics. see Wikipedia: Marginal propensity to consume (Q1879079)
fraction of income increase that is saved. see Wikipedia: Marginal propensity to save (Q1661238)

triples.name: fiscal multiplier
triples.description: the ratio of the change in aggregate demand to the change in government spending that caused it. see Wikipedia: Fiscal multiplier (Q18907327)

triples.name: Phillips curve
triples.description: economic model illustrating an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. see Wikipedia: Phillips curve (Q220841)

triples.name: Laffer curve
triples.description: representation of the relationship between taxation and government revenue. see Wikipedia: Laffer curve (Q504749)

triples.name: Lorenz curve
triples.description: graphical representation of the distribution of income or of wealth. see Wikipedia: Lorenz curve (Q430378)

triples.name: Gini coefficient
triples.description: measure of inequality in income or wealth distribution. see Wikipedia: Gini coefficient (Q162455)

triples.name: Okun's law
triples.description: economic relationship. see Wikipedia: Okun's law (Q595592)
principle in economics. see Wikipedia: Law of demand (Q3875012)
economic theory principle. see Wikipedia: Law of supply (Q3537482)

triples.name: Pareto efficiency
triples.description: economic concept of a state in which no reallocation of resources can make everyone at least as well off. see Wikipedia: Pareto efficiency (Q36829)

triples.name: present value
triples.description: economic concept denoting value of an expected income stream determined as of the date of valuation. see Wikipedia: Present value (Q1100466)

triples.name: future value
triples.description: value of an asset at a specific date. see Wikipedia: Future value (Q610286)

triples.name: net present value
triples.description: valuation in finance. see Wikipedia: Net present value (Q192472)
ratio between the net profit and cost of investment resulting from an investment of some resources. see Wikipedia: Return on investment (Q939134)
measure of the profitability of a business. see Wikipedia: Return on equity (Q591174)

triples.name: return on assets
triples.description: ratio to express the profitability of a company's assets in generating income. see Wikipedia: Return on assets (Q1163221)
average income of an economy. see Wikipedia: Per capita income (Q45918)
methods of estimating the doubling time of an investment. see Wikipedia: Rule of 72 (Q264016)

triples.name: doubling time
triples.description: time required to double a quantity. see Wikipedia: Doubling time (Q1424376)

formulas.name: definition of GDP deflator
formulas.expression: "GDP deflator" = "gross domestic product" / "real gross domestic product" * 100

formulas.name: equation of exchange
formulas.expression: "money supply" * "velocity of money" = "price" * "real gross domestic product"

formulas.name: propensity identity
formulas.expression: "marginal propensity to consume" + "marginal propensity to save" = 1

formulas.name: doubling time by the rule of 72

sources.name: by definition
sources.description: a value that is fixed by an unambiguous definition or convention rather than measured

sources.name: economics textbook example
sources.description: illustrative figures used to demonstrate the calculation of an economic formula
